explanation_md: `r.width` is `5` and `r.height` is `3`. The expression `r.width * r.height` evaluates to `15`. The `'\n'` ends the line, so the output is `15` followed by a newline.

options.a: Line 3: struct name must be lowercase
options.b: Line 7: the closing `}` of the struct definition is missing the semicolon
options.c: Line 11: you cannot use the dot operator on a non-pointer variable

explanation_md: A struct definition must end with `};` (closing brace followed by a semicolon). Without it the compiler cannot determine where the struct definition ends and where the rest of the code begins, producing an error on or after line 8. Options (a), (c), and (d) describe non-issues.

prompt_md: You have two `Point` variables, `a` and `b`. After executing `a.x = 10; b = a; b.x = 99;`, what is `a.x`?

options.a: 99, because `b = a` makes `b` an alias for `a`
options.b: 10, because structs are copied by value, so `b` is an independent copy
options.c: Indeterminate, because `a.x` was not re-assigned after the copy
options.d: 0, because integer members are zeroed when copied

explanation_md: Assigning one struct variable to another copies all member values. `b` becomes an independent copy of `a`. After `b.x = 99`, only `b.x` changes; `a.x` remains `10`. Structs are not reference types — they do not alias each other.
The first `cout` outputs `p.name` ("Bob"), a space, and `p.age` (30), then a newline. The next line increments `p.age` to 31, and the second `cout` prints `31` followed by a newline.
